description:
You are a container operations engineer responsible for automating a very small “rolling-redeploy” workflow for three micro-services that live in the directory
    /home/user/microservices.

    The following items already exist inside /home/user/microservices:
      • services.txt – a plain-text file that lists the exact service IDs, one per line.
      • (nothing else)

    Your goals:

    1. Create the directory /home/user/scripts if it does not yet exist.
    2. Inside that directory, create an executable shell script named
          /home/user/scripts/redeploy_services.sh
       that does ALL of the following when it is run:
         a. Reads each service name (one per line) from /home/user/microservices/services.txt.
         b. For every service read, append a single status line to the file
               /home/user/microservices/redeploy.log
            Note: If redeploy.log does not exist, the script must create it automatically.
         c. The status line format MUST be exactly:
               YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS - <service-name> - OK
            • YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS is the current local time stamp produced by the      command date '+%F %T'
            • The delimiter is a single literal space on both sides of the hyphens.
            • <service-name> is the string taken verbatim from services.txt
            • “OK” is the fixed literal word OK (uppercase).
    3. Execute the script once so that /home/user/microservices/redeploy.log is populated with one line per service.
    4. Display the final contents of /home/user/microservices/redeploy.log to the console so that the automated grader can capture and verify them.

    Implementation constraints:

      • The script may be written using any POSIX-compatible shell features, but it must start with the shebang #!/bin/bash
      • The entire task can be accomplished with no more than four core shell commands inside the script (for example: cat, while/for loop, date, echo).
      • Set the script’s owner execute bit so it can be run directly (chmod u+x).
      • Do NOT hard-code the service names in the script; always read them from services.txt.

    What the grader will check:

      1. /home/user/scripts/redeploy_services.sh exists, is executable, and contains a shebang line.
      2. /home/user/microservices/redeploy.log exists after you run the script.
      3. The log contains exactly one line for every service listed in services.txt (no more, no less).
      4. Every line matches the regex:
           ^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2} [0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2} - (auth|api|front) - OK$
      5. The lines appear in the same order they are listed inside services.txt.

    Reminder: only the contents of /home/user/microservices/redeploy.log printed at the end should appear in your final console output.
truth:
Initial file that already exists before the agent starts:
      • Path: /home/user/microservices/services.txt
        Contents (exact, 3 lines, newline at end of each line):
            auth
            api
            front

    After successful task completion the grader expects:

      1. Script file:
           Path: /home/user/scripts/redeploy_services.sh
           File is executable by owner.
           First line equals: #!/bin/bash

      2. Log file:
           Path: /home/user/microservices/redeploy.log
           Line count = 3
           Each line matches regex:
             ^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2} [0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2} - (auth|api|front) - OK$
           The three lines appear in this exact lexical order: auth first, then api, then front.

    The grader will ignore the actual time stamps as long as they match the above pattern.
